summary: I enlisted about a year after Pearl Harbor, which was a pivotal moment for me. We went in and out of Pearl Harbor several times while I was aboard the USS South Dakota. Every time we passed the USS Arizona, we had to stand at attention out of respect, even before the memorial was built.

I chose to enlist in the United States Marine Corps right after Pearl Harbor because it seemed like a strong branch to be part of. I went through a tough boot camp at Parris Island and was one of the few from my platoon assigned to sea duty rather than the 5th Division Marines. Onboard the USS South Dakota, I served as a Marine manning anti-aircraft guns and was even in charge of the brig at times.

Boot camp at Parris Island was rough but essential training for me. I entered in November 1941 and finished around February 1942. It prepared me for the challenges ahead, especially serving aboard a battleship like the USS South Dakota.

After boot camp, I went to sea school in Norfolk, Virginia, which was fortunate because it prepared me for shipboard life. It was from Norfolk that I boarded the USS South Dakota in March 1943. Norfolk was a key naval hub for us before heading out to sea.

I served aboard the USS South Dakota starting in March 1943 after it had been repaired from the Battle of Santa Cruz Islands. I was part of the Marine contingent, manning the 20-millimeter anti-aircraft guns and earlier the five-inch mounts. The South Dakota was heavily armed and one of the most decorated battleships, serving in both the Atlantic and Pacific theaters, including bombardments of Japan and participation in major battles.
cues: Battle of Santa Cruz Islands aftermath

summary: I wasn't aboard the USS South Dakota during the Battle of Santa Cruz Islands, but I heard it was a tough fight. The ship was badly damaged with bombs hitting turrets, and even one Marine was killed. The captain was wounded but later returned to duty.

During our time in the Atlantic, the USS South Dakota stopped at Scapa Flow in Scotland. From there, we made a feint toward Norway as part of the preparations for the invasion of Africa. It was an important strategic location during the war.

We made a feint at Norway while stationed in the Atlantic, which was part of the larger Allied strategy around the time of the North African invasion. This was during our deployment after leaving Scapa Flow and before heading to Africa.

Operation Torch was the Allied invasion of North Africa, and our feint at Norway was connected to that campaign. Although I wasn't directly involved in the landings, our naval movements helped divert enemy attention. This was early in the war before we shifted focus to the Pacific.

While in the Atlantic, we spent about four or five months operating in the Arctic Circle, which earned us the Royal Order of the Blue Nose. It was a challenging environment, but part of our duties before returning for repairs and then moving to the Pacific theater.

We came in and out of Pearl Harbor Naval Base about three times while I was on the USS South Dakota. The base was busy with reconditioning old battleships, and although the memorial wasn't there yet, we always showed respect passing the USS Arizona. Pearl Harbor was a key staging point for our operations in the Pacific.

Every time we passed the USS Arizona at Pearl Harbor, the entire crew had to stand at attention out of respect for those lost in the attack. At that time, there was no memorial yet, just flags standing. It was a solemn reminder of the war's beginning.

The USS South Dakota participated in the Battle of the Philippine Sea, where Admiral Oldendorf and the battleships decimated one of the Japanese fleets. It was a decisive naval battle that showcased the power of our battleships and cruisers working together. I was aboard during this time, and it felt like poetic justice against the Imperial Japanese Navy.

While I didn't mention Admiral Spruance directly in my experience, he was a key figure in the Pacific theater during the time I served aboard the USS South Dakota. His leadership was instrumental in battles like the Philippine Sea, which we participated in.

Ulithi Atoll served as our base in the Pacific for a time. Even while anchored there, we were attacked, including kamikaze attacks that struck nearby ships like the USS Randolph. It was a critical forward base during the island-hopping campaigns.

While at Ulithi Atoll, we witnessed the USS Randolph being hit by kamikaze planes. It was a shocking moment as we were watching movies on the fantail when the attack happened. The kamikaze threat was very real and dangerous during that period.

Kamikaze attacks were a terrifying part of our experience in the Pacific. I recall the night at Ulithi Atoll when two kamikaze planes attacked, one hitting the USS Randolph. These suicide attacks were deadly and unpredictable, and we had to stay constantly vigilant.

Although I didn't specifically mention fighting at Okinawa, the USS South Dakota was involved in many island battles, including Okinawa. It was one of the last and most intense battles in the Pacific, with heavy kamikaze attacks and bombardments.

The USS South Dakota supported operations around Iwo Jima, providing bombardment and anti-aircraft defense. Iwo Jima was a fierce battle, and our ship was part of the naval forces that helped secure the island for the Marines on the ground.

During the Battle of Formosa, our fleet was bombarding the island when we experienced a mechanical issue that left us dead in the water. The fleet moved ahead without us for a couple of hours while we cleared saltwater from the fuel lines. It was a tense moment but we caught up eventually.

In 1944, we encountered Typhoon Cobra, a massive storm that lasted three days and nearly capsized the USS South Dakota. The storm was so severe that some destroyers capsized because they didn't have time to fill their ballast tanks. It was blamed on Admiral Nimitz for not anticipating the storm, but weather forecasting was limited back then.

Admiral Nimitz was the commander of the Pacific Fleet during my service. He was criticized after Typhoon Cobra for not knowing the storm was coming, but given the technology of the time, it was understandable. His leadership was crucial throughout the Pacific campaign.

I served as a Marine aboard Navy ships, so I was closely integrated with the United States Navy. The Navy handled the ship operations while we Marines manned anti-aircraft guns and the brig. Our cooperation was essential for the success of battles across both the Atlantic and Pacific theaters.

While transferring ammunition from a Liberty ship to the USS South Dakota, we experienced a dangerous explosion below decks. The captain had to order flooding of the compartment to prevent the ship from being lost, even though men were inside. It was one of the most hazardous moments I faced at sea.

I didn't have direct contact with William D. Leahy during my service, but he was a significant figure in the Navy's high command during World War II. His leadership helped shape naval strategy in the early years of the war.

We participated in the Battle of Lady Gulf in the Philippines. The Japanese tried to fool us, but the old battleships, including the South Dakota, came in and took over the fight. It was a tough battle, but we held our ground under Admiral Halsey's command.

Admiral Halsey was aboard the USS South Dakota for a while and brought his whole staff. He was a strong leader and commanded the fleet during some of the toughest battles we faced. His presence boosted morale and effectiveness on the ship.

The USS Missouri was significant because President Truman, who succeeded Roosevelt, was from Missouri. We were supposed to have the Japanese surrender signing on the South Dakota, but it ended up on the Missouri instead. It was a symbol of the war's end and American naval power.

After Roosevelt died, Harry S. Truman became president and took over the war effort. He was from Missouri, which is why the surrender signing happened aboard the USS Missouri instead of the South Dakota. His leadership marked the final phase of the war.

I didn't mention serving on the Franklin, but it was one of the aircraft carriers active during the war. The carriers played a vital role in the Pacific, complementing battleships like the South Dakota with air power.

The USS Enterprise was a famous aircraft carrier during the war. While I served on the South Dakota, the Enterprise was part of many major battles, providing critical air support. It was one of the most decorated ships of the war.

During our bombardments of Japan, we could see the B-29 Superfortress bombers flying overhead. They were a key part of the air campaign against Japan, complementing the naval bombardments from ships like the South Dakota.

entity: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
summary: While I didn't witness the atomic bombings directly, they marked the end of the war shortly after our bombardments of Japan. The bombings were a decisive factor leading to Japan's surrender and the conclusion of our service aboard the South Dakota.

We faced the Imperial Japanese Navy in many battles, including the Philippine Sea and the Battle of Lady Gulf. They were a formidable enemy with skilled sailors and pilots, including kamikaze tactics that made the fighting intense and dangerous.

The F6F Hellcat was one of the main fighter planes used by the Navy and Marines during the war. While I was primarily on anti-aircraft duty aboard the South Dakota, these planes were crucial in gaining air superiority over the Japanese.

The Mitsubishi A6M Zero was the primary Japanese fighter plane we faced. It was fast and maneuverable, and combined with kamikaze tactics, it posed a serious threat to our ships. Our anti-aircraft guns and fighter planes like the Hellcat worked hard to counter them.
